content.problemStatement:
The fuel economy of a car is the distance which it can travel on one litre of fuel.

The base fuel economy (i.e, its fuel economy when there is only one person - the driver - in the car) of a certain car is MMM kilometres per litre. It was also observed that every extra passenger in the car decreases the fuel economy by 111 kilometre per litre.

PPP people want to take this car for a journey. They know that the car currently has VVV litres of fuel in its tank.

What is the **maximum** distance this car can travel under the given conditions, assuming that all PPP people always stay in the car and no refuelling can be done?

**Note** that among the PPP people is also a driver, i.e, there are exactly PPP people in the car.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing three space-separated integers P,MP, MP,M, and VVV - the number of people, base fuel economy, and amount of fuel present in the car, respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output a single line containing one integer - the maximum distance that the car can travel.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers P, M and V — the number of people in the car (including the driver), the base fuel economy (kilometres per litre) when only the driver is present, and the amount of fuel in litres.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer — the maximum distance (in kilometres) the car can travel.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: effective economy = 10 - (5-1) = 6 km/l, distance = 6*20 = 120 km.
Test case 2: effective economy = 6 - (1-1) = 6 km/l, distance = 6*10 = 60 km.
Test case 3: effective economy = 6 - (4-1) = 3 km/l, distance = 3*1 = 3 km.

Test case 1: effective = 30-(2-1)=29, distance=29*7=203.
Test case 2: effective = 20-(3-1)=18, distance=18*15=270.

solution.approach: For each test case the effective fuel economy is reduced by 1 km/litre for every passenger beyond the driver. Hence effectiveEconomy = M - (P - 1). The maximum distance is simply effectiveEconomy multiplied by the amount of fuel V. The computation is O(1) per test case, so overall O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        P = int(next(it))
        M = int(next(it))
        V = int(next(it))
        eff = M - (P - 1)
        out_lines.append(str(eff * V))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
